extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces **real-time data exchange capabilities** to ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, enabling near-instantaneous bidirectional updates. While the document does not specify exact synchronization frequencies (e.g., Hz, ms), the emphasis on **runtime configuration management** implies dynamic adjustments to operational needs, suggesting adaptive temporal granularity for different services (e.g., sensor data, control signals, or analytics). Latency requirements are implicitly tied to the **interactive visualization** and **real-time monitoring/control** use cases, where delays must align with human operational workflows (e.g., Facility Managers interacting with the DT). The framework’s modular architecture supports **standardized extension points**, allowing custom services to define their own temporal constraints (e.g., high-frequency sensor fusion vs. periodic energy analytics).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin framework supports a **centralized architecture** with a **DT Framework** acting as the core hub, coordinating bidirectional data exchange across all instances. Multiple **Digital Twin instances** can coexist, each tailored to specific roles: **Building Control Engineers** deploy device-specific twins using BIM tools, **Facility Managers** interact with operational twins for real-time monitoring, and **DT Engineers** extend capabilities via service integration. The **hierarchical structure** is implied by the life cycle flow from **enriched BIM models** (static representations) to **operational DTs** (dynamic instances), with responsibilities scoped to each role’s domain (e.g., device modeling vs. runtime control). Decentralized coordination is enabled through the framework’s **modular architecture**, allowing custom services to integrate while maintaining system-wide consistency via standardized extension points.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system employs a **coordinated orchestration pattern** centered on the **DT Framework**, which serves as a unifying hub for **bidirectional data exchange** and **holistic building life cycle management**. The architecture follows a **role-based component integration** model, where **Building Control Engineers**, **Facility Managers**, and **DT Engineers** contribute to distinct phases of the DT life cycle (deployment, monitoring, and extension). **Resource management** is implicit in the **runtime configuration management** capability, enabling dynamic adjustments without full redeployment, while **system-wide coordination** is achieved through the framework’s **modular architecture** and **standardized extension points**. The **enriched BIM-to-operational DT flow** (as depicted in Figure 1) ensures alignment between static design data and dynamic operational needs, with the framework facilitating interoperability across heterogeneous components.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The Digital Twin system prioritizes **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** as a non-functional requirement (NF_X), ensuring seamless integration with external tools and platforms. Key integration aspects include:
  - **Data exchange protocols**: Leverages **Eclipse Hono** (for IoT data ingestion) and **Eclipse Ditto** (for digital twin state management) to standardize communication.
  - **Dependencies**: Relies on **BIM tools** (e.g., Revit) for initial device modeling and enriched model deployment, while operational data flows through the DT Framework.
  - **Integration patterns**: Supports **modular service extension**, allowing third-party systems (e.g., enterprise asset management, CMMS) to connect via standardized interfaces. The framework does not specify exact protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, REST APIs) but emphasizes **scalable, low-overhead interoperability** to bridge static BIM representations with dynamic operational needs in the AECO domain.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **BIM (Building Information Modeling)** as its foundational modeling paradigm, enabling the creation of **semantically rich, object-based 3D models** that integrate **geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics** of building components. These models incorporate **relationships between components** (e.g., structural, spatial, and functional dependencies) and support **collaborative design coordination** among architects, engineers, and construction professionals. The system employs a **model-driven automation framework** to bridge **design-time models** (BIM-based) with **runtime operations**, ensuring seamless integration of static (geometric) and dynamic (behavioral/analytical) model types. Data sources include **BIM models** (e.g., IFC or proprietary formats) and **real-time operational data** (via Eclipse Hono and Ditto for synchronization). The architecture is **modular and service-oriented**, with defined **extension points** to support **scalable DT development** in AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations). The framework facilitates **semantic integration** of heterogeneous data sources, enabling interoperability across the building lifecycle (design, construction, operation).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The Digital Twin system supports **operational control mechanisms** for physical infrastructure, enabling remote actuation and real-time adjustments via the DT framework. While specific actuator types (e.g., motors, valves, or lighting controls) are not detailed, the system is designed to interface with **physical assets in AECO contexts**, allowing for automated deployment and reproducible setups (R2). The modular architecture (R4) ensures extensibility for integrating control systems, though explicit technical specifications (e.g., actuation ranges, safety constraints, or communication protocols) are not provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure relies on **Eclipse Hono** for real-time data ingestion and **Eclipse Ditto** for live synchronization between physical assets and their digital twins (R3). The system supports **real-time data integration** from physical components, though specific sensor types (e.g., IoT sensors, environmental monitors) or spatial distribution details are not provided. Data transmission is enabled through these protocols, ensuring seamless connectivity for operational monitoring. No explicit mention of sampling frequencies, accuracy specifications, or data collection mechanisms (e.g., wireless protocols) is included in the document.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin framework ensures **semantic fidelity** through BIM’s object-based modeling, which inherently supports clash detection, performance simulation, and documentation alignment. Validation is implicitly supported by the **real-time synchronization** (via Eclipse Hono/Ditto) and the **modular architecture**, which allows for iterative refinement of models. The system prioritizes **semantic integration** and **operational alignment** between design-time (BIM) and runtime models, though explicit validation methods (e.g., statistical testing, uncertainty quantification) or error correction mechanisms are not detailed. Quality assurance is embedded in the **model-driven deployment process**, ensuring reproducibility and scalability in AECO contexts.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Digital Twin (DT) system facilitates real-time data exchange between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, leveraging enriched Building Information Modeling (BIM) data as the foundational input. Data flows from physical assets (e.g., sensors, IoT devices) to the DT via standardized protocols, including **Eclipse Hono** for device connectivity and **Eclipse Ditto** for digital twin state management. The system supports continuous synchronization, ensuring operational data (e.g., environmental metrics, equipment status) is transmitted at high frequency to maintain an up-to-date digital representation. Preprocessing steps, such as data validation and normalization, are implied to ensure data quality before integration into the DT. The framework’s modular architecture allows for seamless integration of custom analytics and services, enabling dynamic adaptation to evolving operational requirements. Event triggers (e.g., threshold breaches, scheduled updates) further refine data transmission, ensuring only relevant or critical updates are propagated. Data integration mechanisms are designed to bridge static BIM representations with dynamic operational data, enabling a holistic view of the building’s lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Digital Twin (DT) system enables **control flows** from the digital to the physical domain, primarily through the role of **Facility Managers**, who interact with the deployed DT for **real-time monitoring and control** of physical infrastructure (e.g., lighting systems, broader operational systems). The framework supports **runtime configuration management**, allowing dynamic adjustments to control logic without requiring full redeployment. While the document does not specify **command types** (e.g., HVAC adjustments, security protocols) or **control message formats**, the system is designed to ensure **bidirectional data exchange** between the DT and physical assets. **Validation and authentication mechanisms** for control commands are not explicitly described, nor are **feedback systems** or **conflict resolution** strategies detailed. However, the framework’s **modular architecture** implies support for standardized pathways to execute commands, with **command execution confirmation** inferred through the continuous synchronization loop. The absence of explicit technical details (e.g., message schemas, encryption protocols) limits granularity.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin (DT) system’s **technical network infrastructure** is underpinned by a **modular architecture** with standardized extension points, enabling integration of custom services and analytics. The document highlights **real-time data exchange capabilities**, suggesting the use of **Eclipse Hono and Ditto** (though their specific roles—e.g., device connectivity vs. state management—are not defined). The system prioritizes **interoperability across heterogeneous systems**, implying support for **cross-platform communication protocols** (e.g., MQTT, HTTP/REST) and **scalable network topologies** to accommodate components from individual devices to entire infrastructure systems. **Bandwidth and latency requirements** are not quantified, but the emphasis on real-time synchronization implies low-latency, high-throughput pathways. **Reliability and redundancy mechanisms** are not explicitly described, though the framework’s adaptability suggests fault-tolerant design. **Security protocols** for network communication are implied by the need for interoperability and data integrity but are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The Digital Twin (DT) framework supports scalable deployment models, with a modular architecture enabling integration of custom services and analytics. While the document does not explicitly mention hosting platforms (e.g., cloud, edge, or on-premise), the emphasis on **scalability** and **runtime configuration management** suggests adaptability to varying deployment scenarios. Computational and storage requirements are tailored to the operational needs of the AECO domain, with low-overhead updates ensuring maintainability. The framework’s central hub role facilitates bidirectional data exchange, implying a cohesive deployment strategy, but specific details about availability, reliability assurance, or deployment models (e.g., hybrid) are not provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the proposed model-driven deployment framework spans **five core phases**, addressing the gap between static Building Information Modeling (BIM) representations and dynamic DT requirements. These phases include:
  1. **Device Modeling**: Building Control Engineers define **sensors, actuators, and controllers** directly within the BIM environment, enriching static BIM models with **semantic representations** of Building Control System (BCS) components. This phase establishes a **unified semantic foundation** for subsequent transformations.
  2. **Model Transformation**: Leveraging **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles**, the framework extracts **structured representations** from enriched BIM models and **automatically generates deployable DT artifacts** with minimal manual intervention. This phase ensures seamless transition from static BIM to dynamic DT representations.
  3. **DT Deployment**: The generated artifacts are deployed, enabling real-time synchronization between the physical building and its digital twin. This phase includes **initialization of control logic** and **integration of core DT services** as defined by Building Control Engineers.
  4. **Operation and Monitoring**: Facility Managers interact with the DT to **monitor system states** and **operate physical infrastructure** via the twin. This phase emphasizes **real-time data exchange** and **interactive visualization** for immersive monitoring and control.
  5. **Evolution and Extension**: DT Engineers extend functionality through **custom services and plugins**, integrated via the framework’s **modular architecture** with standardized extension points. This phase supports **runtime evolution** of the DT to adapt to changing operational requirements.

  **Transition Management**: The framework ensures **automated synchronization** between phases, particularly between device modeling (BIM) and DT deployment, using MDE-driven transformations. **Lifecycle governance** is implicit in the roles defined (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers), each responsible for distinct phases, ensuring accountability and consistency. The modular architecture further supports **version control** and **evolution management** by isolating custom services and analytics from core DT functionality.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The **twinning process** in the proposed framework follows a **model-driven approach**, integrating **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles to bridge the gap between static BIM representations and dynamic Digital Twin (DT) requirements. Key aspects include:
  - **Methodologies**: The process employs **semantic enrichment of BIM models** to define **sensors, actuators, and controllers**, creating a **unified semantic foundation** for DT deployment. MDE-driven **model transformations** automate the extraction of structured representations, reducing manual intervention.
  - **Quality Assurance**: The framework ensures **consistency** between physical and digital representations through **real-time data exchange** capabilities, synchronized via automated synchronization mechanisms.
  - **Testing and Validation**: While not explicitly detailed, the **automated generation of deployable DT artifacts** implies rigorous validation of transformations to ensure functional correctness. The **modular architecture** with standardized extension points facilitates **incremental testing** of custom services and plugins.
  - **Version Control**: The **modular design** allows for **isolated updates** to custom services and analytics, enabling **versioned evolution** of the DT without disrupting core functionality. The framework’s **extension points** support **backward compatibility** and **controlled updates**.
  - **Evolution Management**: The DT evolves through **runtime integration of custom services** and **analytics**, managed via the **modular architecture**. This approach enables **adaptive extension** of DT capabilities to meet changing operational needs, with **Facility Managers** and **DT Engineers** overseeing deployment and customization.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The Digital Twin (DT) system supports **decision-making capabilities** through **real-time monitoring, interactive visualization, and analytics**, though specific details on analytics types or processing capabilities are not provided. Key features include:
  - **Types of Insights**: The framework enables **system state monitoring** (e.g., sensor data, actuator status) and **operational insights** derived from real-time data exchange between the physical building and its digital twin. **Interactive visualization** provides immersive interfaces for **Facility Managers** to assess building performance.
  - **Decision-Making Processes**: Decisions are informed by **real-time data synchronization** and **visualized insights**, allowing Facility Managers to **operate physical infrastructure** via the DT. The **modular architecture** supports integration of **custom analytics services**, enabling domain-specific decision support (e.g., energy optimization, fault detection).
  - **User Interaction Methods**: Interaction occurs through **immersive visualization interfaces**, tailored for monitoring and control. The **role-based access** (e.g., Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers) ensures context-aware decision support.
  - **Confidence Measures**: While not explicitly detailed, the **automated synchronization** and **real-time data exchange** imply high confidence in the accuracy of insights, assuming reliable sensor/actuator data. Custom services may incorporate **validation mechanisms** to assess the reliability of analytics outputs.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework adheres to **AECO-specific standards** (e.g., **BIM**) while addressing **interoperability and modularity challenges** through **standardized extension points**. **(1) Industry Standards**: The solution is built upon **BIM as a foundational standard**, enabling seamless integration with **Building Information Modeling (BIM) tools** and **Building Control System (BCS) components**. **(2) Compliance Frameworks**: The framework’s **modular architecture** incorporates **standardized extension points** to ensure compatibility with **third-party services and plugins**, mitigating the lack of standardized integration frameworks in traditional BIM-DT deployments. **(3) Interoperability Standards**: While specific standards (e.g., IFC, OPC UA) are not explicitly named, the **automated MDE pipelines** and **real-time data exchange** imply adherence to **industry protocols for bidirectional communication** between physical and digital systems. **(4) Certification Requirements**: No explicit certification requirements are detailed, but the **role-based governance model** (e.g., Building Control Engineers, DT Engineers) suggests alignment with **industry best practices for DT deployment and operation**. The framework’s **semantic enrichment of BIM models** ensures **consistent data representation**, addressing the **static limitations of BIM** while maintaining compliance with **AECO workflows**.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the 'Living Lab case study' mentioned in the abstract as the system under study. It accurately describes the context as a 'LEED Platinum-certified academic building' based on the reference to Gerber et al. [33] in the Related Work section. The focus on 'energy efficiency, indoor environmental quality, and operational optimization' aligns with the paper's themes in the AECO domain. However, the specific listing of 'Sensors (unspecified types)' and 'Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) framework' as *physical* components is slightly conflated; MDE is the methodology proposed, not a physical component, though the extraction correctly notes its role. The reasoning regarding suitability is well-supported by the paper's discussion on the 'practical application... in a Living Lab'.

The extracted services are strongly supported by the text. The abstract explicitly states the DT enables 'real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization'. It also mentions 'rich, interactive visualization' via Unreal Engine. The text references Gerber et al. [33] regarding 'monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis' and Opoku et al. [34] regarding optimizing 'indoor environmental conditions'. While the extraction lists specific capabilities like 'Prescriptive maintenance', which is not explicitly detailed in the snippets (though 'decision-making capabilities' and 'facility management' are mentioned), the core categories (monitoring, simulation, visualization, optimization) are accurate and well-grounded.

This extraction is very precise and directly grounded in the source text. The abstract explicitly states the framework 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'. The Related Work section mentions 'Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31]' as open-source frameworks and 'Bentley’s iTwin.js [32]' in the context of AECO. The roles assigned to these tools in the extraction (e.g., real-time data exchange, visualization) match the descriptions in the text perfectly. The inclusion of 'Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)' is also central to the paper's title and content.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

It also accurately connects resource management to 'runtime configuration management' and cites the 'modular architecture' as the coordination mechanism.

It accurately notes the 'standardized extension points' for modular service integration. However, it incorrectly lists 'Revit' as a specific dependency, as the source text only mentions 'BIM tools' in general and does not specify Revit.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
